---
title: "The end-to-end workflow"
---

Whatever the data source, a SciGraphs project follows the same five-stage
pipeline. This page is the conceptual backbone; each stage links to the panels
that implement it.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
    A["1 Import or Generate"] --> B["2 Analyze"]
    B --> C["3 Lay Out"]
    C --> D["4 Visualize"]
    D --> E["5 Render & Export"]
```

## 1. Import or generate a graph

Load a file or database, download an OSMnx network, build an urban morphology
graph, or pull a SuiteSparse matrix. Source detection and column mapping are
handled in the **[Data panel](../panels/scigraphs/data.qmd)**; spatial sources
have their own tabs ([OSMnx](../panels/osmnx/index.qmd),
[City2Graph](../panels/city2graph/index.qmd)).

The result is a single Blender object carrying the graph as mesh topology
(vertices = nodes, edges = edges) plus custom properties such as `num_nodes`,
`num_edges`, and `is_directed`.

## 2. Analyze

Compute centrality, community structure, statistics, and topology. Results are
stored as **named mesh attributes** on the graph object, which become available
to colour and size mappings later. See the
**[Analysis panel](../panels/scigraphs/analysis.qmd)** and the
**[Graph Algorithms panel](../panels/scigraphs/algorithms.qmd)**.

This stage is optional but generally precedes visualization, because the metrics
it produces inform the visual encoding.

## 3. Lay out

Arrange nodes with any of the 2D/3D force-directed, spectral, geometric, or
Graphviz layout algorithms — or skip layout entirely when the data carries
intrinsic coordinates (geospatial, sparse-matrix). See the
**[Layout & Positioning panel](../panels/scigraphs/layout.qmd)**.

## 4. Visualize

Apply Geometry Nodes via **Setup Visual**, then drive node/edge colour and size
from attributes using scientific colormaps and edge-style presets. See the
**[Visualization panel](../panels/scigraphs/visualization.qmd)**.

## 5. Render & export

Render with Cycles or EEVEE, and export the graph (GEXF, GraphML, JSON, CSV,
Pajek), node positions, or a statistics report. See the
**[Export & Tools panel](../panels/scigraphs/export.qmd)**.

## Reproducing the whole pipeline

SciGraphs can replay an entire pipeline — dataset, analysis, layout,
visualization, render, and export — from a short declarative JSON or YAML file:

```json
{
  "meta": { "title": "complex_demo", "seed": 42 },
  "dataset": { "source": "osmnx", "method": "PLACE", "query": "Burjassot, Valencia, Spain", "network_type": "drive" },
  "analysis": { "metrics": ["degree", "betweenness"] },
  "layout": { "algorithm": "YIFAN_HU", "scale": 8.0 },
  "visual": { "node_color": "betweenness", "node_size": "degree", "colormap": "magma", "edge_style": "GEPHI_DEFAULT" }
}
```

Each run emits a canonical specification, a provenance manifest (with
input/output hashes and timing), and an execution log, so the same seed and
inputs reproduce the same result. See the
**[Reproducibility panel](../panels/scigraphs/reproducibility.qmd)** and the
**[reproducible-pipeline tutorial](../tutorials/05-reproducible-pipeline.qmd)**.
